metadata.topic: The ship launched as Empress of Japan (II), later Empress of Scotland, then Hanseatic

title: RMS Empress of Japan (1930-1942) — project summary
text: The second Canadian Pacific ship named RMS Empress of Japan was built by Fairfield Shipbuilding and Engineering Company at Govan for trans-Pacific service. Launched on 17 December 1929 and completed in June 1930, she served the Vancouver, Yokohama, Kobe, Shanghai, Hong Kong route during the 1930s. Her prewar identity is the project’s primary Empress of Japan (II) context for passenger lists, menus, plans, and shipboard interpretation.

metadata.source_license_status: facts summarized by project team from cited reference
metadata.topic: Empress of Japan (II) prewar Pacific service

title: RMS Empress of Scotland (1942-1957) — project summary
text: In 1942 the same hull was renamed RMS Empress of Scotland. After wartime service, she was rebuilt between 1948 and 1950 for colder North Atlantic conditions, including a reworked passenger configuration. This identity explains why museum records for the Empress of Japan (II) also include Scotland-era material such as later menus and accommodation records tied to the same physical vessel.

metadata.source_license_status: facts summarized by project team from cited reference
metadata.topic: Empress of Scotland postwar refit and service

title: TS Hanseatic (1958-1966) — project summary
text: After Canadian Pacific sold the vessel in 1958, Hamburg-Atlantic Line rebuilt and renamed her TS Hanseatic. The ship served as a German liner and cruise ship until a 1966 engine-room fire in New York caused severe damage. She was towed to Hamburg, judged beyond economic repair, and sold for breaking up, closing the life of the hull launched as Empress of Japan.

metadata.source_license_status: facts summarized by project team from cited reference

title: Empress of Japan 1934 baseball voyage — project summary
text: One notable prewar voyage connected the Empress of Japan with the 1934 American baseball tour of Japan. Baseball players including Babe Ruth crossed the Pacific aboard the ship, with stops including Honolulu and Yokohama. This contextual source is useful for interpreting celebrity travel, passenger culture, and the ship’s place in trans-Pacific public imagination during the 1930s.

metadata.source_license_status: facts summarized by project team from cited reference; source page not bulk-ingested
